speaker: interviewer
text: --Hirsch, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Fredy Hirsch was quite an extraordinary man, a tremendous gymnast, and a sort of-- outgoing, laughter-- personality. Laughter was around him always. Everything was a joke. He took a view of the camp the first role call in the morning. And we all called out our names so that he would get acquainted with us. And we were there about three days or four days already.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many children were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there must have been about five-- perhaps a hundred-- perhaps more-- all in this one-- one-- hut. And we were all six, seven-- that sort of thing. And when I said, [NON-ENGLISH] Honigwachs, and he said, oh, one of the royal family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he immediately explained-- to my chagrin-- he immediately explained that every-- that we were six brothers. And every one of the brothers was [NON-ENGLISH] in Novy Jicin. And you look at [NON-ENGLISH] Honigwachs-- and I was then six. He will one day be [NON-ENGLISH], Novy Jicin. [LAUGHS] And I never actually-- [LAUGHS] --got to that, because the war intervened. What I remember then was about two or three days later, we played something which we called a wide game, which was the red army against the blue army. And we all had bands on our sleeves. And since I was the smallest, I was in-- I was a runner to the general staff of the blue army. And the general of the blue army was Fredy Hirsch, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we all went. The general staff went to one side of a valley. It's very beautiful countryside, you know, meadows in the middle-- sort of a gentle valley with a brook running through it, and forests-- small forests-- to either side so that there would be cover. You'll see who could attack from the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was up to the generals, you know, [LAUGHS] to plan their campaigns. But we gathered on the ridge on one side and the red army on the other side. And you could see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rule of procedure was that if somebody would come and tear your ribbon, you'd be dead. And you then had to sit down where you were, because you'd be dead, you see? And then, there would be a reckoning of who won the war. So I was going to be Fredy's runner, because I was too small to be in the army. And incidentally, the grown-ups, who were leading us, were also allowed to kill the kids, you see? So there was no age differentiation. Everybody was fighting for themselves. So you had-- if a grown-up was chasing you, you'd have to run from them. So I was the runner. And Fredy was explaining, you know, we're going to attack from this side and from that side.

speaker: interviewee
text: He spoke only German, by the way, Fredy. He came from Aachen. He was born in Aachen. And he came to Czechoslovakia in 1933 as a refugee from Aachen. And you see how fate is. He only ran as far as Czechoslovakia. But he became leader of Maccabi Hatzair. Well, he was waving his arms about. And as he was waving his arms about, he struck my ribbon and actually tore it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was dead. So I started crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was-- this was bitter, you know, because the war hadn't started yet. I was dead already, you see? So they took a piece of straw. And they said, we'll call you injured.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sent me on an errand. And I ran as fast as I could. And one of the invigilators of this war saw me running with this straw. And he said, you're dead! Sit down. You're not allowed to go wherever you're going. So I said, I'm not dead. I'm injured.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I continued running. Well, we had a whole-- after the game was over, there was a whole sitting, you know. The whole camp had to decide whether it was justified that I was injured or whether I was dead. And the whole camp decided, dead is dead, you know? I was disqualified.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] Because I ran on after being told that I was dead. So that's how I met Fredy Hirsch, the second time. I met him when he came to our house. But I didn't hardly speak to him, because I was a little squirt. And this was a grown man. But I spoke to him quite a bit at the camp. Fredy-- perhaps I should tell you about the fate that my brother, [NON-ENGLISH] had with Fredy in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the leadership of Maccabi Hatzair. In 1939, the leadership had to-- they were sending the last-- the Germans were already in Prague. And they were sending the last group of halutzim who had been on [NON-ENGLISH], you know, who-- preparation to go on kibbutzim in Palestine. And they were taking the last group to cross illegally into Hungary through Slovakia, and then from Hungary into Romania, and so on. And they were embarking somewhere on the Dead-- on the Black Sea, and sailing to Haifa. I mean, that's a story all of its own how this was organized. But when the leadership discussed who should lead the [NON-ENGLISH]. [HEBREW] in Hebrew means a colonel. And this was the colonel that was going to start a kibbutz in Israel, or Palestine then. So they decided one of the leaders-- there were two leaders who shared the national leadership-- Fredy Hirsch and my brother, [NON-ENGLISH]. And they had to decide who will go to Palestine and who will lead the movement in-- in occupied Czechoslovakia. And they did it by drawing the matches. They broke a match. And whoever got the broken match stayed. And my brother got the-- he chose the right match.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was the leader of the [HEBREW] that went to Palestine. And he went to Palestine. And I remember in '39 coming-- we were still able to travel-- although the Germans were already in occupation of Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Prague with my mother and with [NON-ENGLISH]. And we said goodbye to [NON-ENGLISH] when he departed. He already was then engaged to [NON-ENGLISH], who became his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is quite amusing. She wanted to leave Czechoslovakia because of-- she saw what was happening. And she was a young woman of our town. She wasn't in the youth movement at all, and the only way to leave was through the youth movement. So she came to the headquarters. And she said, well, who is in charge here of this group that's going? So they said, well--

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTING] --she want to see [NON-ENGLISH] Honigwachs. And she says, well, I saw [NON-ENGLISH] Honigwachs, and I made eyes with him. And I became his girlfriend, and that's how I got to Palestine. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she's now his widow. Going back to the German occupation, what do you remember of that event?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, ask me. I remember almost everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's--

speaker: interviewee
text: I could probably tell you--

speaker: interviewer
text: --outstanding?

speaker: interviewee
text: --sometimes day by day happenings. One of the stories that I gave you, written up, about the children called [NON-ENGLISH], which was published in which is the journal of the Jewish Genealogical Society of Great Britain. I'm one of the six founders of this society. It's rather surprising how I know the name of them being [NON-ENGLISH], the whole-- the story is written up. We better just refer to it, because it's a long and involved story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you tell me about your involvement in the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: My involvement is that my mother sent me, shortly before we were going to Terezin, on the 15th of September, 1942-- '42? '43? Yes, '42. I always have to think, because you don't remember dates. You remember the sequence. I was sent with a letter to the president of the community. And I had forgotten the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sent you with the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. And she said, give it into his hand. So it was important. And my mother sent me, because I was a kid. Oh, and she took my star off. So I knew it was something. And something weird was going on. I already knew. The something weird which was going on, which I wasn't in on it, but I only learnt it by the time we got to Theresienstadt. We had-- you know, I told you how my mother was helping people. When we were in Valasske Mezirici and we got-- we fled in November 1938 from Novy Jicin, 15 kilometers south into Czechoslovakia proper, because Novy Jicin became part of the Reich. So we fled. And we started living in Valasske Mezirici. When we lived there, people would come from Poland. And although we were refugees, and we only had a one room flat, and there were three of us, there were people staying with us two or three times a week. They'd stay overnight. And they'd disappear the next day. And my mother would always give them cash, sometimes papers. I don't know how she organized that. So we knew that was-- [NON-ENGLISH] and I knew that was not to be talked about. And these people disappeared. But then in the summer of 1942 came this gorgeous redheaded woman. Oh, she was a young woman of about 25-- buxom. She was a redhead-- very jolly sort of-- and she'd tell-- told terrible stories about Poland. They all told terrible stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, the Holocaust was in full swing. I mean, the gas chambers weren't in full swing, but the murders were going-- were being done everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you heard these stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's interesting that-- I mean, I heard them. And I didn't hear them. I'm now wondering. I heard the stories. And I knew, you know, that these people are telling stories. Because they just want my mother to put her hand deeper into the pocket. Because they are out for sympathy. That's how I saw it. I never discussed it with my mother or with-- with [NON-ENGLISH]. I knew why I didn't discuss it with my mother, because once when-- when-- when I was quite small, a beggar came to our front door. The doorbell went. So I ran downstairs. And my sandek, you know, the man who holds you when you're being circumcised, was a man called [NON-ENGLISH]. And he gave me two crowns for my birthday. Two crowns was a lot of money for a little boy. And I had two crowns in my pocket. And this beggar came to the front door. And I ran downstairs-- opened the front gate. The front gate was locked, incidentally, because we had a break-in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I unlocked-- saw it was a beggar. And I saw what you do with a beggar. I put my hand in my pocket, and I gave him my two crowns. And I went back upstairs. And my mother says, who was it? So I said, it was a beggar. And she said, well, where is he? So I said, well, I've already seen to him. So she says, what do you mean you've seen to him? She was getting angry already. So I said, well, I had some money. So I gave it to him. So she says, what money? You know, she had forgotten that I had two crowns. So I said, you know. So she says, you mean, you gave him two crowns? And I said, yes. I knew there was something wrong, because she was very angry already. And then I said that I gave him two crowns.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the first time I got-- I got-- my face slapped. I mean, I can still feel the slap. It was such a-- such a-- because I didn't do anything naughty. And she said, when people come to this house, giving alms is my duty, not yours. And you're being punished, because you gave him two crowns. Because now he'll go into the pub, and he'll get himself drunk. You give a beggar 25 hellers. You don't give two crowns. Because a male beggar will go to the pub. And he'll get drunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew when my mother was giving alms or doing something, it wasn't my-- [LAUGHS] place to ask questions. Because I remembered the slap. You were talking about the redheaded woman that came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the redheaded woman. Well, the redheaded woman, she was the only one out of all these Polish Jewish people that came through who disappeared one day. And I didn't ask anymore. She's gone. She stayed a longish time. And then, she disappeared. But five or six days later, she came back. And she's the only one that came back. And Leo told me in Theresienstadt what the situation was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you briefly tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the situation was that my mother sent this woman with Czech papers-- the Czech papers were organized through the president of the Jewish community-- to actually walk through the ghetto. Because she knew or heard that there's a road, a public road, going through the ghetto, which is true. There was such a road through Theresienstadt. And she went to a place called Leitmeritz, or Litomerice, in Czech. She took a train. And she got her-- she hitched a ride with a-- with a farmer. And she went through the ghetto. There were fences either side. And she got off and spoke to some people and asked a few questions. And she got back. What she told my mother, I don't know. I do remember once my mother talking with her in the evening-- something that I wasn't supposed to hear-- about my mother's abortion. My mother had an abortion. She should have had a seventh child. And I was born when she was 42. So the seventh child would be when she was about 45 or something. I think she had a fallen womb or something. There was some problem. And she had-- she couldn't have another child. So she had an abortion. And she went-- I remember when she went. Because she left the house to go to a rabbi in [NON-ENGLISH] to get a [NON-ENGLISH], because her life was in danger. And then-- I'm digressing. It's a-- it's a whole interesting story about my mother, because I overheard these two women talking-- women's talk. I wasn't supposed to listen, you know. I was supposed to be asleep. But-- [LAUGHS] we were all in one room. She was in bed with my mother. And I was in bed with [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was straining to-- [LAUGHS] --to hear what was being said. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else in your family was at home at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, [NON-ENGLISH], I, my mother-- three-- one room. We had a piano from-- from home-- [LAUGHS] --that was taking up a quarter of the room. So then, there were two beds. And, I mean, you couldn't walk two steps without falling over something, because we went from a huge house that we had in Novy Jicin to this pokey little room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to England. He was in England. And he came to England in January 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he manage to get to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: He just-- there's a passport. He came to England as a-- as a-- he was a major merchant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason that you and your mother didn't come with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because he wanted to transfer a large amount of money. So he bought furs-- precious furs this time-- in Russia. And he had them transported to England. And he came to take them out of store and sell them in London. And that was his capital to save us. I remember, my father was a-- an operator. He was-- he was a very, very astute man. Unfortunately, my mother just didn't live up to that. She knew she was different. She has a different view of life from him. He was cosmopolitan. And he was-- he knew political situations. And he could outguess, you know, what to do with money, how to conduct oneself, and so on. My mother couldn't outguess anything. She was duped. I mean, we were all duped. And my mother wasn't stupid. She was not. But she was not-- my father had an intellect, and he also had that sort of manner, you know, that he knew what was happening around the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, were you experiencing any greater degree of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not amongst the Czech in Valasske Mezirici. No. I mean, we knew to keep out of the way of the Germans. And we were already wearing the star. And there were various things, which were forbidden. Like I wasn't allowed to go to school and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1942, I was 13. But 1940, I was 11 when I would-- when I wasn't allowed to continuing going to school. Anyway, this woman comes back. And I'm sent with this letter. Well, I knew it was something to do with this woman. But my mother apparently wrote to the president that there was a conversation going on between this girl, Zlata, with people in Theresienstadt. And apparently, nobody's being killed there. So we can go. And I carried the letter. At the time that I was carrying it, I knew it was something confidential and something that I mustn't get caught with. But I didn't know what it was at the time. I could find out only later when I was about 13, 14. And you-- you-- you know, maturity comes very quickly in-- in not only circumstances, but also age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you take the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took it to the president's house. And I come to a villa. We were refugees, you see. This is president. He was-- he was-- he was at home. He had a villa. And I saw a lady of the house had a lovely garden. And a gardener was wrapping roses in straw. And it was sort of early September-- lovely weather-- hot. And he was wrapping roses in straw. And there were two little girls playing on a swing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go and knock on the-- I asked the gardener, is this the house of Dr. so-and-so? I forgot the name throughout the time. I'll tell you how I discovered the name. Anyway, I asked, is Dr.-- now I know it's Dr. Heller. Is Dr. Heller at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, I knew it then. So she says, no, I'm Mrs. Heller. You can give it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, no. I have to give it to Dr. Heller personally. So she smiled. And she actually had it-- as she came to open the door, she had a tray with Ribena. You know that sort of-- Blackcurrant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blackcurrant juice, you know, for the girls in the garden. And it was so hot. And I had ran up, up this hill to where the villa was. The villa isn't there anymore. The Czechs destroyed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she says, would you like something to drink? You know, I must have looked at the-- [LAUGHS] at the drink. So she gets a third glass. There was enough there. She said, take it in the garden. You can have a drink with them. So I took it to these-- these little girls. And we all had this Ribena juice, blackcurrant juice. And then, we swung on the swing. And I swung a bit. And these little girls were too little to swing. You know, a child has-- you know how you have to learn to swing? You have to tuck in under. And you couldn't even teach them. I mean, they were little squirts of kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was two or something. And the other one was-- one was just a baby. She hardly walked. So you had to swing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I swung them for a bit. Then, I got bored. So I went to the gardener. And I asked him, what are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he answers me in high-speaking Czech. But he answers me in German. It's quite unusual in Valasske Mezirici, because Valasske Mezirici is a Czech town. And he says to me in German, [GERMAN]. The lord and lady of the house, or the Mr. and Mrs. of the house are departing. [GERMAN], you refer to nobility. You know, that's what [GERMAN] means in German. But [GERMAN] is also politely saying, the exalted people, you know, are leaving for a while. He said for a while. I knew we were leaving, because we already had our call-up papers. It didn't mean much to me. You know, we were leaving. In fact, as a kid, I thought, it was so boring in this town. I didn't go to school. And we were hemmed in, you know. Leaving is better than staying, because something new is going to happen. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I sort of remember him replying, you know, that he's wrapping up these roses for the winter. So I thought, ah, well, we-- we will be staying over the winter. And Dr. Heller came back, and I gave him the letter. And on the 12th, we went to Theresienstadt. We went to Theresienstadt. There was a curfew. The Germans had a method for everything. The method was that there's a curfew-- no Czechs are in the streets. You walk at night, not to the railway station, but to a siding way outside the railway station behind a park.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop the tape here. And then, we'll talk some more on the next tape.
